---
title: Apps
description: Apps are the platform capabilities Templates are built with. Build one to extend what agents can do and ship it alongside a Template.
---

import { Cards, Card } from 'fumadocs-ui/components/card';

<p className="eyebrow">For developers</p>

Apps are the component layer Templates are built from. A Template bundles one or more Apps; an App is a self-contained TypeScript module that provides a capability — a web UI, an MCP tool surface, local SQLite state, and a lifecycle the runtime manages.

If you're here to build and publish a Template — not a platform capability — start at **[Templates](/docs/build/templates)** instead. You probably don't need this section.

If you want to extend the platform with a new capability (a new integration, a custom MCP server, a novel module), this is the right place.

## Quick start

Create a workspace-local App and get the runtime to pick it up.

### 1. Lay out the folders

```text
<workspace-root>/
  workspace.yaml
  apps/
    my_app/
      app.runtime.yaml
      package.json
      src/
```

### 2. Install dependencies

```bash title="terminal"
cd apps/my_app
npm install
```

### 3. Write the app manifest

Start with a minimal `app.runtime.yaml`:

```yaml title="apps/my_app/app.runtime.yaml"
app_id: my_app
name: My App
slug: my-app

lifecycle:
  setup: npm install
  start: npm run start

healthchecks:
  mcp:
    path: /mcp/health
    timeout_s: 30
    interval_s: 5

mcp:
  transport: http-sse
  port: 13100
  path: /mcp/sse
  tools:
    - create_record

env_contract:
  - HOLABOSS_WORKSPACE_ID
```

Rules that matter immediately:

- `app_id` must match the workspace declaration exactly
- `mcp.port` is required by the current parser
- use either `lifecycle.start` or top-level `start`, not both

### 4. Register the app in the workspace

```yaml title="workspace.yaml"
applications:
  - app_id: my_app
    config_path: apps/my_app/app.runtime.yaml
```

Duplicate `app_id` values fail compilation.

### 5. Start through the runtime

```bash title="terminal"
# Run lifecycle.setup
curl -X POST http://127.0.0.1:5160/api/v1/apps/my_app/setup

# Run lifecycle.start
curl -X POST http://127.0.0.1:5160/api/v1/apps/my_app/start

# Poll build status
curl http://127.0.0.1:5160/api/v1/apps/my_app/build-status

# See the resolved port map
curl http://127.0.0.1:5160/api/v1/apps/ports
```

When `mcp.tools: [create_record]` is declared, the runtime reconciles `workspace.yaml` so the allowlisted tool id becomes `my_app.create_record`.

## What a healthy app looks like

The runtime probes these surfaces; any failure keeps the app from being marked healthy.

- `GET /` on the app port returns `2xx` or `3xx`
- `GET <healthchecks.mcp.path>` on the MCP port returns `200`
- `app_id` in `app.runtime.yaml` matches `workspace.yaml`
- `mcp.port` and `mcp.path` are explicit

If setup fails, pull the latest diagnostics:

```bash title="terminal"
curl 'http://127.0.0.1:5160/api/v1/apps/my_app/setup-log?workspace_id=workspace-1'
```

## Runtime endpoints you'll use

| Endpoint | Purpose |
| --- | --- |
| `POST /api/v1/apps/:appId/setup` | Run setup lifecycle |
| `POST /api/v1/apps/:appId/start` | Start the app |
| `GET /api/v1/apps/:appId/build-status` | Poll setup/build status |
| `GET /api/v1/apps/:appId/setup-log` | Fetch setup diagnostics |
| `GET /api/v1/apps/ports` | Resolved app ↔ port map |
| `POST /api/v1/apps/install` | Install from files |
| `POST /api/v1/apps/install-archive` | Install from archive (serialized per `(workspace_id, app_id)`) |
